one_line_takeaway: PPFT trains an LLM decoder to condition directly on k-pooled, Laplace-noise-obfuscated client-side embeddings instead of raw prompt tokens, achieving markedly better inversion-attack resistance (near-zero attribute recall for age/antecedent, ROUGE-L below 0.25 at epsilon=75) than d_chi-privacy, paraphrasing, and PrivacyRestore baselines while retaining up to 95.6% of noise-free task accuracy on legal QA with an 8B decoder.
key_finding: With the Llama-3.1-8B decoder, PPFT achieves the best average downstream accuracy among privacy-preserving methods: 0.7314 average (Pri-DDX 0.5915, Pri-NLICE 0.6979, Pri-SLJA 0.9049), versus PrivacyRestore 0.6343, Paraphrase 0.3757, and d_chi-privacy 0.2750; the noise-free upper bound is 0.9291 and the no-domain-adaptation lower bound is 0.3545. With the 1B decoder PPFT scores 0.5699 average versus PrivacyRestore 0.4519, Paraphrase 0.2635, and d_chi-privacy 0.2608 (upper bound 0.7573). On the legal Pri-SLJA set specifically, PPFT reaches 95.6% task accuracy with the 8B model and 85.0% with the 1B model, close to the noise-free upper bound. On general-domain tasks (Table 4), the 8B PPFT model scores 0.5278 on CSQA and 0.7085 on SQuAD versus a noise-free upper bound of 0.6086/0.8930 (drops of 0.081 and 0.184), while d_chi-privacy and Paraphrase baselines collapse (e.g., d_chi-privacy 0.1819/0.0174 on CSQA/SQuAD). For privacy robustness, PPFT keeps ROUGE-L below 0.25 even at epsilon=75, and on Pri-DDX attribute-level recall it achieves 0.0071 for age and 0.0115 for antecedent (near-zero) versus PrivacyRestore's 0.3552 (symptom) and 0.3317 (antecedent); PPFT's sex recall (0.5894) is close to the 0.5 random baseline for a binary attribute, similar to PrivacyRestore's 0.5642. Ablations show pooling size k trades off performance against reconstruction resistance (k=4: score 0.9049 / ROUGE-L 0.4050; k=16: score 0.7630 / ROUGE-L 0.3241 on Pri-SLJA), and Laplace noise gives consistently lower ROUGE-L (stronger resistance) than Gaussian noise across epsilon values.
core_contribution: The paper proposes PPFT (Privacy-Preserving Fine-Tuning), a two-stage training pipeline that lets a client interact with a cloud-hosted LLM without ever transmitting raw prompt text. Stage 1 (Encoder-Decoder Alignment) trains a client-side encoder (ModernBERT-large) together with a server-side projection layer and a LoRA-adapted decoder (Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct or Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct) so the decoder can generate conditioned on block-wise mean-pooled ('k-Pooling') continuous embeddings instead of discrete tokens. Stage 2 (Text-free Domain Adaptation) fine-tunes only the server-side projection layer and decoder on domain-specific (medical/legal) data using embeddings that have been further obfuscated with isotropic Laplace noise (calibrated under a d_chi-privacy interpretation, direction sampled uniformly from the unit sphere and magnitude from a Gamma distribution, followed by L2 re-normalization). At inference, the client applies the same encode -> k-pool -> noise-inject pipeline and transmits only the obfuscated embeddings; the server never sees plaintext or reconstructable token-level detail, and the client never needs access to the server's decoder parameters.
threat_model: An honest-but-curious or compromised MLaaS server (or an eavesdropper on the transmission channel / a party with post-hoc access to logs) attempts to recover the original natural-language prompt or specific sensitive attributes (age, sex, symptoms, antecedents) from the embeddings it receives. The attacker is modeled concretely as a generative inversion adversary: it pretrains a reconstruction model on clean (non-obfuscated) embeddings and then applies it to the obfuscated embeddings actually transmitted at a given privacy budget epsilon, measuring reconstruction quality with ROUGE-L against the true prompt and with attribute-level recall for four sensitive fields. The paper does not model attacks on the encoder itself, model-extraction of the server LLM, or poisoning of Stage-2 fine-tuning data; the focus is purely input-confidentiality against reconstruction/inversion and attribute-inference attacks under an MLaaS deployment.
limitations: The paper's own text notes: (1) ROUGE-L values on Pri-SLJA can appear high in absolute terms because many samples share a long, standardized legal instruction prefix, so partial-prefix recovery is easier even when the rest of the prompt is poorly reconstructed -- an acknowledged confound in their privacy metric. (2) The pooling size k explicitly trades downstream performance against reconstruction resistance, meaning there is no setting that maximizes both simultaneously. (3) The threat model covers only generative embedding-inversion and attribute-inference attacks under a defined privacy budget; the paper does not address adaptive attackers with knowledge of the specific noise mechanism/parameters, model-extraction of the server decoder, or integrity/poisoning risks in Stage-2 adaptation. (4) The client-side encoder is not fine-tuned in Stage 2 and its embedding quality is fixed by Stage-1 alignment, which the paper does not stress-test against domain shift beyond the two evaluated domains (medical, legal).

quantitative_results: Llama-3.1-8B average downstream score: PPFT 0.7314 vs PrivacyRestore 0.6343 vs Paraphrase 0.3757 vs d_chi-privacy 0.2750; upper bound (no noise) 0.9291; lower bound (no Stage2) 0.3545
Llama-3.2-1B average downstream score: PPFT 0.5699 vs PrivacyRestore 0.4519 vs Paraphrase 0.2635 vs d_chi-privacy 0.2608; upper bound 0.7573; lower bound 0.3788
Attribute recall on Pri-DDX: PPFT age 0.0071, sex 0.5894, symptom 0.1001, antecedent 0.0115 vs PrivacyRestore sex 0.5642, symptom 0.3552, antecedent 0.3317
General-domain (8B): PPFT CSQA 0.5278 / SQuAD 0.7085 vs upper bound 0.6086 / 0.8930 (drops of 0.081 and 0.184)
General-domain (1B): PPFT CSQA 0.5125 / SQuAD 0.6579 vs upper bound 0.543 / 0.7303 (drops of 0.030 and 0.072)
ROUGE-L reconstruction stays below 0.25 for PPFT even at epsilon=75
Pooling-size ablation on Pri-SLJA: k=4 score 0.9049 / ROUGE-L 0.4050; k=8 score 0.8363 / ROUGE-L 0.3553; k=16 score 0.7630 / ROUGE-L 0.3241
Laplace noise yields consistently lower ROUGE-L than Gaussian noise across tested epsilon values
baselines_compared: PPFT w/o stage2 (lower bound, no domain adaptation)
PPFT w/o noise (upper bound, no privacy noise)
